landscape.title: Ukraine and the Netherlands v. Russia
landscape.filedDisplay: First filing 13 March 2014; merits hearing 12 June 2024
landscape.abstract: The largest inter-state case in ECHR history. Four applications consolidated covering eastern Ukraine, the children abductions, MH17, and the full-scale invasion.
detail: Unanimous violations under Articles 2, 3, 4 § 2, 5, 8, 9, 10, 11, 13, 14, and 38 of the Convention, and Articles 1 and 2 of Protocol No. 1.
Separate violations of Articles 2, 3, and 13 in respect of MH17 (app. 28525/20).
Just satisfaction adjourned. MH17 application disjoined for separate processing.
Article 46 measures: release of detainees pre-16 September 2022; cooperation in identifying transferred children.
landscape.outcome: Russia found responsible for patterns of violations across eight years and four conflict phases.

landscape.title: Ukraine v. Russia (re Crimea)
landscape.abstract: The companion inter-state case on the occupation and annexation of Crimea from February 2014.
detail: Violations of Articles 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 14, and 18 of the Convention.
Russian law could not be regarded as 'law' within the meaning of the Convention as imposed in occupied territory.
Decided unanimously.
landscape.outcome: Russia found responsible for an administrative practice of violations in occupied Crimea.

landscape.title: Four pending Ukraine v. Russia inter-state applications
landscape.abstract: Per ECHR PR 173 (2025), four further Ukraine v. Russia inter-state applications and approximately 9,500 individual applications remain pending, related to events in Crimea, eastern Ukraine, the Sea of Azov, and Russia's military operations from 24 February 2022.
detail: The exact composition of the four pending applications is not enumerated in the public press release.
Approximately 9,500 individual applications relating to the conflict are also pending.
Russia's cessation as Contracting Party on 16 September 2022 limits the temporal scope to acts occurring up to that date.
landscape.outcome: Pending. Future judgments expected; the Committee of Ministers continues to supervise execution of decided cases.

landscape.title: Allegations of Genocide under the Genocide Convention
landscape.decidedDisplay: Provisional measures 16 March 2022; preliminary objections 2 February 2024
landscape.abstract: Ukraine seeks a declaration that it did not commit genocide in Donetsk and Luhansk, and challenges Russia's invocation of genocide as a justification for the use of force.
detail: Provisional measures order, 16 March 2022: Russia ordered to suspend its military operations in Ukraine. Vote: 13–2.
Preliminary objections judgment, 2 February 2024: most Russian objections rejected; subject-matter jurisdiction over use-of-force claims declined 12–4.
Merits track on Ukraine's request for a declaration that no genocide occurred continues.
32 States have filed declarations of intervention.
landscape.outcome: Pending on the merits. Russia has not complied with the provisional measures.

landscape.title: Appeal Relating to the Jurisdiction of the ICAO Council under Article 84 of the Chicago Convention
landscape.parties: Russia v. Australia and the Netherlands
landscape.abstract: Russia's appeal of the ICAO Council's 12 May 2025 decision finding it breached Article 3 bis of the Chicago Convention by use of weapons against MH17.
detail: Filed under Article 84 of the Chicago Convention in conjunction with Article 36(1) of the ICJ Statute.
Russia called the ICAO Council decision 'illegitimate' and refused to accept it.
Pending before the ICJ.

landscape.title: Situation in Ukraine
landscape.parties: Office of the Prosecutor v. multiple Russian officials
landscape.filedDisplay: Investigation opened 2 March 2022 (after 41 State referrals); jurisdiction backdates to 21 November 2013
landscape.abstract: Ongoing ICC investigation into war crimes, crimes against humanity, and genocide on the territory of Ukraine.
detail: 17 March 2023: arrest warrants for Vladimir Putin and Maria Lvova-Belova for unlawful deportation and transfer of Ukrainian children. Rome Statute Articles 8(2)(a)(vii) and 8(2)(b)(viii).
5 March 2024: arrest warrants for Lieutenant General Sergei Kobylash and Admiral Viktor Sokolov for missile strikes on Ukrainian energy infrastructure.
24 June 2024: arrest warrants for former Defence Minister Sergei Shoigu and Chief of the General Staff Valery Gerasimov on the same conduct.
First ICC arrest warrant ever issued against the leader of a permanent member of the UN Security Council.
landscape.outcome: Investigation ongoing. No arrests; Russia is not a State Party to the Rome Statute.

landscape.title: MH17 — Article 84 Dispute before the ICAO Council
landscape.parties: Australia and the Netherlands v. Russia
landscape.abstract: First decision on the merits of an Article 84 dispute in the Council's 80-year history. Russia found to have breached Article 3 bis of the Chicago Convention.
detail: Article 3 bis prohibits the use of weapons against civil aircraft in flight.
Vote: 22 in favour, 3 against, 10 abstentions.
Triggers binding reparations talks with the Netherlands and Australia.
Russia has appealed the decision to the ICJ.
landscape.outcome: Russia found responsible. Russia rejected the decision and appealed to the ICJ.

landscape.title: Detention of Three Ukrainian Naval Vessels
landscape.abstract: Following the Kerch Strait incident of 25 November 2018, Russia seized the Ukrainian naval vessels Berdyansk, Nikopol, and Yani Kapu and detained 24 servicemen. Ukraine instituted Annex VII proceedings; ITLOS handled provisional measures.
detail: Vessels: Berdyansk, Nikopol, Yani Kapu. 24 detained Ukrainian crew members.
Order, 25 May 2019: Russia ordered to release the vessels and their crews. Vote: 19–1 on each measure.
Russia did not appear at the hearing; argued the dispute concerned military activities and was thus excluded under UNCLOS Article 298(1)(b).
Vessels and crew were subsequently returned. The Annex VII merits arbitration continued at the PCA.
landscape.outcome: Russia complied (under continued international pressure) and released the vessels and crew.

landscape.title: Coastal State Rights in the Black Sea, Sea of Azov, and Kerch Strait
landscape.abstract: UNCLOS Annex VII arbitration concerning Russia's interference with Ukrainian rights in the Black Sea, Sea of Azov, and Kerch Strait — including the seizure of subsoil resources, fishing rights, and obstruction of transit.
detail: Tribunal constituted 29 November 2016.
Award on Russia's preliminary objections, 21 February 2020: jurisdictional challenges partially upheld and partially rejected.
Russia argued that questions of sovereignty over Crimea fell outside the Tribunal's UNCLOS jurisdiction.
Merits proceedings continue.

landscape.title: Detention of Ukrainian Naval Vessels and Servicemen — Annex VII Merits
landscape.abstract: The Annex VII tribunal merits proceedings on the Kerch Strait incident, following ITLOS provisional measures.
detail: Russia raised preliminary objections on military-activities and law-enforcement exemptions under UNCLOS Article 298.
Award on Preliminary Objections, 27 June 2022, partially upheld jurisdiction.
Merits phase continues.

landscape.title: MH17 Criminal Trial
landscape.parties: Netherlands Public Prosecution Service v. Girkin and others
landscape.abstract: Trial of four named suspects for the downing of MH17 on 17 July 2014. Held at the Schiphol Judicial Complex (JCS), Badhoevedorp, by the District Court of The Hague.
detail: Convicted in absentia, life imprisonment: Igor Girkin, Sergey Dubinskiy, Leonid Kharchenko.
Acquitted: Oleg Pulatov (the only defendant represented in court).
Court found that MH17 was hit by a Buk missile fired from a farm field near Pervomaiskyi, Ukraine.
The Joint Investigation Team (Netherlands, Australia, Belgium, Malaysia, Ukraine) underpinned the prosecution.
landscape.outcome: Three convictions in absentia. Verdict final.

landscape.title: Naftogaz and others v. Russia
landscape.parties: NJSC Naftogaz of Ukraine and 6 group companies v. Russia
landscape.abstract: UNCITRAL/PCA arbitration under the Russia–Ukraine BIT (1998) for the unlawful expropriation of Naftogaz's Crimean oil-and-gas assets following the 2014 annexation. The largest Crimea-related award against Russia.
detail: February 2019 jurisdictional ruling: tribunal upheld jurisdiction over Crimean assets and found Russia in breach of the BIT.
Final award on quantum, 12 April 2023: approximately USD 5 billion in damages and interest.
Enforcement orders obtained in the United Kingdom (5 December 2023), France, Austria, the Netherlands, and the United States.
Russia has refused to pay voluntarily; Naftogaz has been attaching frozen Russian assets in multiple jurisdictions.
landscape.outcome: Russia found liable for ~$5 billion. Enforcement under way; partial collection.
landscape.enforcementNote: Recognition orders in UK/FR/AT/NL/US; partial attachment of frozen Russian state assets.

landscape.title: Oschadbank v. Russia
landscape.parties: Joint Stock Company State Savings Bank of Ukraine v. Russia
landscape.abstract: UNCITRAL arbitration seated in Paris under the Russia–Ukraine BIT for the expropriation of Oschadbank's Crimean banking infrastructure.
detail: Final award, 26 November 2018: approximately USD 1.1 billion in damages, plus interest, bringing total claims above USD 1.3 billion.
Paris Court of Appeal upheld the award on 1 July 2025, dismissing Russian challenges to arbitrator independence.
Oschadbank has separately filed new arbitration claims for assets seized in four further regions following the 2022 invasion.
landscape.outcome: Russia found liable for ~$1.3 billion (with interest). Award upheld on appeal in France.
landscape.enforcementNote: Award upheld by Paris Court of Appeal, July 2025.

landscape.title: Everest Estate LLC and others v. Russia
landscape.parties: Everest Estate LLC and 18 other Ukrainian investors (Privat Group) v. Russia
landscape.abstract: PCA-administered UNCITRAL arbitration concerning the expropriation of Crimean real-estate holdings owned by the Privat Group of Ukrainian investors.
detail: Final award, 2 May 2018: approximately USD 139–150 million in damages, fees, and costs.
First award on the merits in the Crimean BIT-arbitration cluster.
Set-aside proceedings in the Dutch courts (the seat) ultimately upheld the award.
landscape.outcome: Russia found liable for ~$150 million. Enforcement pursued in multiple jurisdictions.
landscape.enforcementNote: Set-aside proceedings unsuccessful in the Netherlands.

landscape.title: Stabil and others v. Russia
landscape.parties: Stabil LLC, Crimea Petrol LLC, Elefteria LLC, Novel Estate LLC and others v. Russia
landscape.abstract: PCA-administered arbitration under the Russia–Ukraine BIT for the expropriation of petrol stations and related operations in Crimea.
detail: 26 June 2017 jurisdictional ruling (issued in parallel with PJSC Ukrnafta v Russia): the BIT applied to annexed Crimea and Russia was responsible for the protection of the investors' Crimean investments.
Final award, 12 April 2019: approximately USD 34.5 million in damages.
landscape.outcome: Russia found liable for ~$34.5 million across the petrol-station claimant group.

landscape.title: PJSC Ukrnafta v. Russia
landscape.parties: Public Joint Stock Company Ukrnafta v. Russia
landscape.abstract: PCA-administered UNCITRAL arbitration under the Russia–Ukraine BIT for the expropriation of Ukrnafta's petrol-station network in Crimea.
detail: 26 June 2017: tribunal held that the Russia–Ukraine BIT applied to annexed Crimea and that Russia was responsible for protecting the investments.
Final award, 12 April 2019: approximately USD 55 million in damages.

landscape.title: PrivatBank and Finilon v. Russia
landscape.parties: PJSC CB PrivatBank and Finance Company Finilon LLC v. Russia
landscape.abstract: UNCITRAL arbitration under the Russia–Ukraine BIT for the alleged expropriation of PrivatBank's Crimean banking infrastructure, including a major ATM network and real-estate assets.
detail: Partial Award, 4 February 2019: tribunal upheld jurisdiction and found that Russia had breached Article 5 of the BIT (expropriation).
Quantum reserved for a later phase of the proceedings.
Underlying claim cited approximately USD 1 billion in invested capital and ~USD 200 million in directly seized assets.
Set-aside proceedings in the Dutch courts.

landscape.title: Naftogaz v. Gazprom — gas supply & transit
landscape.parties: NJSC Naftogaz of Ukraine v. PJSC Gazprom
landscape.decidedDisplay: Gas Sales final award 22 December 2017; Transit final award 28 February 2018
landscape.citation: SCC Arbitration nos. V2014-078-080 and V2014-129
landscape.abstract: Two parallel commercial arbitrations at the Stockholm Chamber of Commerce on the gas-supply and gas-transit contracts between Ukraine's state energy company and Gazprom.
detail: Gas Sales arbitration (V2014-078-080): final award 22 December 2017 — Naftogaz ordered to pay Gazprom for unbought volumes; some contract clauses revised in Naftogaz's favour.
Gas Transit arbitration (V2014-129): final award 28 February 2018 — Gazprom ordered to pay USD 4.63 billion for breaching its minimum transit obligations.
After netting, Gazprom owed Naftogaz approximately USD 2.56 billion.
The Svea Court of Appeal in Sweden has dismissed multiple Russian challenges to the awards.
landscape.outcome: Net award of ~$2.56 billion to Naftogaz. The first major commercial-arbitration win against a Russian state-owned entity from this conflict.
landscape.enforcementNote: Partial enforcement; followed by attachments of Gazprom assets in Europe.

landscape.title: Register of Damage for Ukraine
landscape.parties: Council of Europe (43 participating States and Ukraine)
landscape.filedDisplay: Established 12 May 2023; announced Reykjavik Summit 17 May 2023
landscape.abstract: Records claims for damage, loss, or injury caused by Russia's internationally wrongful acts on or after 24 February 2022. First component of a planned three-part compensation mechanism.
detail: Resolution CM/Res(2023)3, Enlarged Partial Agreement.
Headquartered in The Hague.
Over 80,000 claims received.
Architecture: (1) Register; (2) International Claims Commission [planned]; (3) Compensation Fund [planned].
landscape.outcome: Operational. Future compensation mechanism under negotiation.

categoryDeck.state-responsibility: Holds State A responsible for State B's conduct under treaty law.
categoryDeck.individual-criminal: Holds named individuals criminally liable for international crimes.
categoryDeck.property-commerce: Money damages for expropriation under bilateral investment treaties and commercial contracts.
categoryDeck.maritime-territory: Sovereignty-of-the-sea regime: vessel detention, coastal rights, transit.
categoryDeck.reparations: Mechanisms designed to convert findings into actual compensation.

forums.remit: Inter-state and individual applications under the European Convention on Human Rights.
Disputes between States. Sits at the Peace Palace.
Individual criminal responsibility for the most serious crimes of international concern.
UN agency on civil aviation. Council adjudicates Article 84 disputes under the Chicago Convention.

forums.full: International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea
forums.remit: Disputes under UNCLOS. Hears provisional-measures requests where the Annex VII tribunal is not yet constituted.
Acts as registry for inter-state arbitrations, including UNCLOS Annex VII tribunals.
Dutch national court of first instance; tried the MH17 criminal case at Schiphol JCS.

forums.full: Council of Europe — Register of Damage for Ukraine
forums.remit: Records claims for damage caused by Russia's internationally wrongful acts since 24 February 2022. Registry in The Hague; Council of Europe seat in Strasbourg.

forums.full: Arbitration Institute of the Stockholm Chamber of Commerce
forums.remit: Administers commercial arbitrations seated in Stockholm — including the Naftogaz–Gazprom gas-supply and transit disputes.
